content: read reviews/1132.md. if the summary section of the checklist review indicates the review passed, do nothing. if it failed, make relevant updates that are needed to deepmind/1132.lean accordingly. if in doubt, reference CHECKLIST.md, which was the source document used when a previous instance of claude produced reviews/1132.md.

    46→- [x] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` keyword: both `erdos_1132` (line 72) and `erdos_1132.variants.part2` (line 90).

    50→- [x] Main theorem and variant have docstrings.
    51→- [x] Variant docstring references Bernstein [Be31].
    52→
    53→### `answer()` usage
    54→
    55→The original problem asks yes/no questions: Part 1 asks "must there exist x..." and Part 2 asks "is it true that...". Both are **Case 1**.
    56→

    60→- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)` (both `∀ (seq : ℕ → ℝ)` appear on the RHS of `↔`).

    79→- [x] No `sorry` in `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations.

Both the main theorem (Part 1: existence of a point exceeding the logarithmic barrier infinitely often) and the variant (Part 2: limsup a.e. condition) correctly use `answer(sorry) ↔ P` for yes/no research open problems. Definitions are properly documented, marked `noncomputable` where needed, and follow naming conventions. The only minor observation is that `firstN` could be left computable, but this is harmless.

file.content:
# Review: deepmind/1132.lean — Erdős Problem 1132

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` at line 17.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (line 19).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 1132`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link: `[erdosproblems.com/1132](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1132)`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (Lagrange basis polynomials, Lebesgue function, limsup condition).
- [x] Bibliography: references Bernstein [Be31].

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos1132 ... end Erdos1132` (lines 42–96).
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos1132`.
- [x] `open` statements appear **before** the namespace block: `open Finset BigOperators Filter MeasureTheory` at line 40.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] Helper definitions have LaTeX docstrings:
  - `lagrangeBasis` (line 44–45): docstring present.
  - `lebesgueFunction` (line 49): docstring present.
  - `firstN` (line 53): docstring present.
  - `L` (line 56–57): docstring present.
  - `ValidSeq` (line 60): docstring present.
- [x] Definitions involving classical choice / nonconstructive operations are marked `noncomputable`: `lagrangeBasis`, `lebesgueFunction`, `firstN`, `L` all marked. (`ValidSeq` is a `Prop`-valued `def`, correctly left computable.)
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] Types and Props use `UpperCamelCase`: `ValidSeq`.
- [x] Functions returning types use `lowerCamelCase`: `lagrangeBasis`, `lebesgueFunction`, `firstN`, `L`.
- [x] No problem-specific notation needed; none introduced. N/A.
- [x] No `@[category test]`/`@[category API]` sanity checks added (optional).

> **Minor note:** `firstN` is marked `noncomputable` but its body (`fun i => seq i.val`) is purely computational. This is harmless but unnecessary.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` keyword: both `erdos_1132` (line 72) and `erdos_1132.variants.part2` (line 90).
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_1132` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` conventions.
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 26 41]`.
- [x] Main theorem and variant have docstrings.
- [x] Variant docstring references Bernstein [Be31].

### `answer()` usage

The original problem asks yes/no questions: Part 1 asks "must there exist x..." and Part 2 asks "is it true that...". Both are **Case 1**.

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P` for both theorems.
- [x] Both problems are open, so `answer(sorry)` is correct (not `answer(True)` / `answer(False)`).
- [x] The RHS formalizes the question as originally asked.
- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)` (both `∀ (seq : ℕ → ℝ)` appear on the RHS of `↔`).

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Value is valid: `research open` for both theorems.
- [x] Both `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number is listed: `26 41`.
- [x] Numbers are in **ascending numeric order**: 26 < 41.
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] Both 26 and 41 are valid AMS MSC2020 codes.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Open conjectures use `by sorry` (multi-line form with `by` at end of statement line and `sorry` indented on next line — acceptable per checklist).
- [x] No `sorry` in `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for open research problems.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant is named `erdos_1132.variants.part2` (correct naming convention).
- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research open, AMS 26 41]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining it is Part 2 (the limsup a.e. version) and references Bernstein [Be31].

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `∀`, `∃`, `∈`, `ℕ`, `ℝ`, `→`, `≥`, `∃ᶠ`, `∀ᵐ`, `∏`, `∑`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (longest ≈ 89 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` — not verified in this review (requires build environment).
- [ ] All five custom linters pass — not verified (requires build environment).

---

## Summary

**Result: PASS** (modulo build verification)

The file is well-structured and conforms to all stylistic and structural requirements of the checklist. Both the main theorem (Part 1: existence of a point exceeding the logarithmic barrier infinitely often) and the variant (Part 2: limsup a.e. condition) correctly use `answer(sorry) ↔ P` for yes/no research open problems. Definitions are properly documented, marked `noncomputable` where needed, and follow naming conventions. The only minor observation is that `firstN` could be left computable, but this is harmless.

content.text: The review result is **PASS**. No changes needed to `deepmind/1132.lean`.
